_meta.title: State & Territory Adult + Youth Gambling Prevalence — defensible page snippets
_meta.description: One record per U.S. jurisdiction (50 states + DC + 5 territories + US national reference) with epidemiologist-reviewed, citation-bound prevalence snippets for ProblemGamblingPrevention.com state pages. Drafted by a team of review subagents strictly from verified data; numbers are not to be altered.

rendering_contract.use: Render snippets.body in the state-page body and snippets.sidebar in the sidebar. snippets.youth_sentence and snippets.adult_sentence may be used independently.
never: Do NOT combine or compare the youth and adult figures — youth = past-year PARTICIPATION %, adult = past-year PROBLEM gambling %; different constructs.
Do NOT rank or league-table states.
Do NOT drop the instrument+year or the measured/estimated label from any displayed %.
Do NOT display a number for territories (stat_type "no data") or imply the national estimate applies to them.

stat_type.modeled estimate: national anchor applied to jurisdiction population — label as an estimate, not a measurement
stat_type.no data: no prevalence data; show the unavailability snippet

snippets.sidebar: National benchmarks: adults ~2.2% past-year problem gambling (Williams/Volberg/Stevens 2012); youth ~17.9% past-year gambling participation (Lancet Public Health 2024). Used as anchors for state-level modeled estimates.
snippets.body: The United States national reference figures provide the anchors used to model state-level estimates where no state-specific survey exists. For adults, Williams, Volberg & Stevens (2012) established a standardized past-year problem gambling prevalence of 2.2% (range 1.0-2.7%) across the U.S. adult population. For youth, a 2024 Lancet Public Health systematic review and meta-analysis found a pooled past-year gambling participation rate of 17.9% (95% CI 14.8-21.2) among adolescents. These two figures measure distinct constructs and must not be compared directly to one another.
snippets.youth_sentence: The national benchmark for past-year gambling participation among adolescents is 17.9% (95% CI 14.8-21.2), drawn from a 2024 Lancet Public Health systematic review and meta-analysis pooling 366 samples across 68 countries; this figure represents participation, not problem gambling.
snippets.adult_sentence: The national benchmark for past-year problem gambling among adults is 2.2% (range 1.0-2.7%), with approximately 1.0% meeting criteria for gambling disorder, based on Williams, Volberg & Stevens (2012) standardized prevalence analysis; this figure serves as the modeling anchor for states lacking their own surveys.

youth.source: Lancet Public Health 2024 systematic review & meta-analysis (past-year adolescent gambling)
youth.caveat: National anchor for modeled youth estimates. Past-year PARTICIPATION (not problem gambling). Youth problem-gambling context ~2.1% (SOGS-RA, Welte 2008), range 2-8% by instrument. Do NOT compare youth participation to adult problem-gambling rates.

adult.source: Williams, Volberg & Stevens (2012) standardized US rate
adult.caveat: National anchor used for modeled state estimates. NGAGE '8%' is a behavioral-risk indicator, NOT a disorder rate.

sources.label: Williams, Volberg & Stevens (2012) - Standardized US problem gambling prevalence
Lancet Public Health (2024) - Adolescent gambling participation meta-analysis

data.epi_review_note: Both figures are presented strictly as national reference anchors, not as state-specific measurements. The adult and youth metrics measure different constructs (problem gambling disorder vs. any participation) and are displayed separately to prevent misleading cross-comparison.

snippets.body: No Alabama-specific youth gambling survey has been identified; applying the Lancet Public Health 2024 adolescent meta-analysis (17.9%, 95% CI 14.8-21.2), an estimated ~17.9% of Alabama adolescents may have gambled in the past year. Similarly, no statewide adult problem-gambling prevalence study has been identified for Alabama; applying the national anchor (Williams/Volberg/Stevens 2012), an estimated ~2.2% of adults experience past-year problem gambling. These two figures measure fundamentally different things — youth participation in gambling versus adult gambling disorder — and are not directly comparable. Both are modeled estimates, not measurements from a state survey.

snippets.sidebar: Youth (measured): 52% past-year gambling participation — AYS composite incl. loot boxes, 2024. Adult (measured): 8.0% PGSI severe-risk (est. ~4% disorder) — AZ Dept of Gaming/NORC, 2023.
snippets.body: In the 2024 Arizona Youth Survey (AYS), 52% of youth reported past-year gambling using a broad composite measure that includes spending on in-game features and loot boxes; this figure is not comparable to narrower gambling measures used in other states. In a 2023 statewide survey conducted for the Arizona Department of Gaming (Marotta/NORC; PGSI; adults 21+), 8.0% of adults scored in the severe-risk range, with approximately 4% estimated to meet criteria for gambling disorder. These two figures measure different things — youth participation in gambling versus adult gambling risk — and are not directly comparable. The adult survey used an online panel (AmeriSpeak) and was limited to adults 21 and older, and the youth composite definition is broader than most comparable state surveys.
snippets.youth_sentence: In the 2024 Arizona Youth Survey (AYS), 52% of Arizona youth reported past-year gambling using a broad composite measure that includes spending on in-game features and loot boxes; this figure is not directly comparable to narrower single-item gambling measures used in other states.
snippets.adult_sentence: In a 2023 statewide survey commissioned by the Arizona Department of Gaming (PGSI; AmeriSpeak panel; adults 21+), 8.0% of Arizona adults scored in the severe-risk range on the PGSI, with approximately 4% estimated to meet criteria for gambling disorder.

youth.instrument: AYS composite (incl. paid game features/loot boxes)
youth.source: Arizona Youth Survey (AYS)
youth.caveat: CAUTION: broad composite incl. spending on game features/loot boxes; NOT comparable to narrower state items. 2022 = 54%. (est. affected = 52% (composite) x ~553,405 ages 12-17)

adult.caveat: Measured statewide survey. 21+ frame; AmeriSpeak panel. (count = 8.0% x 5,848,310 adults)

sources.label: Arizona Youth Survey (AYS) 2024
AZ Dept of Gaming (Marotta/NORC) 2023

data.epi_review_note: Both measures are from state surveys and properly labeled 'measured,' but both carry significant caveats: the youth AYS composite includes loot boxes and paid game features, inflating participation relative to conventional gambling items, while the adult PGSI severe-risk rate (8.0%) reflects a broader risk category than the conventional problem+pathological rate, and the 21+ age frame and panel mode add further uncertainty. Each caveat is surfaced in the body snippet.

snippets.sidebar: Youth (estimated): ~17.9% past-year gambling participation (Lancet meta-analysis, 2024). Adult (measured, dated): 1.3% past-year problem+pathological — CA DADP/Volberg NODS, 2006.
snippets.body: No California-specific statewide youth gambling survey has been identified; applying the Lancet Public Health 2024 adolescent meta-analysis (17.9%, 95% CI 14.8-21.2), an estimated ~17.9% of California adolescents may have gambled in the past year. In a 2006 statewide survey conducted for the California Department of Alcohol and Drug Programs (NODS; Volberg), 1.3% of adults reported past-year problem or pathological gambling; this is the most recent published state prevalence figure, though it predates the expansion of online and mobile wagering. These two figures measure different things — youth participation in gambling versus adult gambling disorder — and are not directly comparable. The adult estimate's 2006 vintage is a material limitation for current planning purposes.
snippets.youth_sentence: No California-specific statewide youth gambling survey has been identified; applying the Lancet Public Health 2024 meta-analytic pooled estimate (17.9%, 95% CI 14.8-21.2), an estimated ~17.9% of California adolescents ages 12-17 may have gambled in the past year.
snippets.adult_sentence: In a 2006 statewide survey conducted for the California Department of Alcohol and Drug Programs (NODS; Volberg), 1.3% of California adults reported past-year problem or pathological gambling; this study is now dated and a more recent California Health Interview Survey (CHIS 2023) found that 6.7% of active gamblers screened positive on the Brief Biosocial Gambling Screen (BBGS), a different and not directly comparable metric.

snippets.sidebar: Youth (estimated): ~17.9% past-year gambling participation (Lancet meta-analysis, 2024). Adult (measured, very dated): 2.5% current problem+pathological — CO Dept of Revenue/Volberg SOGS, 1997.

The 1997 vintage of the adult estimate is a significant limitation for interpreting current population burden.

snippets.sidebar: Youth (measured): 25.4% past-year gambling participation — CT School Health Survey (CSHS), 2019. Adult (measured): 1.8% past-year problem+pathological — CT DMHAS/Volberg/Williams PPGM, 2024.
snippets.body: In the 2019 Connecticut School Health Survey (CSHS/CT-YRBS gambling item), 25.4% of high-school students in grades 9-12 reported gambling in the past year, compared with 18.6% in the 2017 wave. In a 2024 statewide survey conducted for the Connecticut Department of Mental Health and Addiction Services (PPGM; Volberg/Williams), 1.8% of adults reported past-year problem or pathological gambling (a parallel NODS analysis yielded 1.4%). These two figures measure different things — youth participation in gambling versus adult gambling disorder — and are not directly comparable. The youth figure is from a 2019 survey of grades 9-12 only and may not reflect current rates following the expansion of legal sports wagering in Connecticut.
snippets.youth_sentence: In the 2019 Connecticut School Health Survey (CSHS/CT-YRBS gambling item), 25.4% of Connecticut high-school students in grades 9-12 reported gambling in the past year.
snippets.adult_sentence: In a 2024 statewide survey conducted for the Connecticut Department of Mental Health and Addiction Services (PPGM; Volberg/Williams), 1.8% of Connecticut adults reported past-year problem or pathological gambling.

data.epi_review_note: Connecticut has measured data for both youth (CSHS 2019, 25.4%, grades 9-12) and adults (PPGM 2024, 1.8%), making this the strongest data quality entry in the cluster. The youth figure is from 2019 (pre-sports-betting expansion), which is a relevant temporal caveat surfaced in the body snippet.

snippets.sidebar: Wyoming: no state-specific gambling prevalence survey identified. Adult (~2.2%) and youth (~17.9%) figures are modeled estimates only, derived from national benchmarks applied to Wyoming's population.
snippets.body: No Wyoming-specific adult or youth gambling prevalence study was identified in the source literature. The adult estimate of approximately 2.2% past-year problem gambling is a modeled figure derived by applying the national anchor from Williams, Volberg & Stevens (2012) to Wyoming's adult population of approximately 454,508; it is not drawn from a Wyoming survey. Similarly, the youth estimate of approximately 17.9% past-year gambling participation is modeled from the Lancet Public Health 2024 adolescent meta-analysis (95% CI 14.8-21.2) and is not from a state-specific survey. Establishing Wyoming-specific surveillance would be required to produce a measured prevalence figure.
snippets.youth_sentence: No Wyoming-specific youth gambling survey was identified; applying the national adolescent past-year participation benchmark of ~17.9% (Lancet Public Health 2024 meta-analysis) to Wyoming's youth population yields an estimated ~17.9% past-year participation, or roughly 7,699 youth ages 12-17, but this is a modeled estimate, not a state measurement.
snippets.adult_sentence: No Wyoming-specific adult gambling prevalence survey was identified; applying the national past-year problem gambling benchmark of ~2.2% (Williams, Volberg & Stevens 2012) to Wyoming's adult population yields an estimated ~2.2%, or roughly 9,999 adults, but this is a modeled estimate, not a state measurement.

snippets.body: No adult or youth gambling prevalence data are available for American Samoa. The source surveillance systems — including state gambling surveys, BRFSS gambling modules, and the national modeling framework (Williams, Volberg & Stevens 2012 for adults; Lancet Public Health 2024 for youth) — do not extend to U.S. territories. No estimate, measured or modeled, can be produced from the available data. Establishing territory-specific gambling surveillance would be the path to producing a local prevalence estimate.
